Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I mostly ended up cramming when things got urgent, listening to lecture recordings while doing other stuff around my apartment. I'd have these bursts of motivation where I'd study for like five hours straight, then nothing for days. It wasn't organized at all, really.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I wish I could say I had a consistent schedule, but that would be a lie. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly realize an assignment was due and panic-study. The only somewhat regular thing was listening to audio materials during my commute or while cooking, but even that was inconsistent.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes a classmate from the peer support group would message me asking if I'd seen something, and that would remind me to log in. I rarely logged in just out of curiosity or genuine interest, if I'm being honest. It was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in every few days, but it never stuck. The peer support program helped a bit because my partner would check in on me, but even then, logging into the LMS never felt like a natural habit. It always felt like a chore I had to remind myself to do.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could replay parts I didn't understand and listen while doing other things. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts in simpler terms than the professor. Everything else felt kind of overwhelming or unnecessary.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and extra materials. There were so many PDFs and supplementary resources that I just couldn't keep up with all of them. I'd download them thinking I'd get to them later, but later never came. The quizzes I'd often delay until the last possible moment because they stressed me out too much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If it seemed like busywork or if the connection to my grade was unclear, I'd push it aside. Also, if my peer support buddy said something was helpful, I was more likely to actually do it.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Like, my brain automatically categorized them as "nice to have" and then I never had the energy for nice-to-haves. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached. Without that pressure, my motivation just wasn't strong enough to make me engage with optional stuff.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download the audio lectures and listen to them on my phone offline, sometimes multiple times. I also had a notebook where I'd write things down while listening, and I'd study from those notes instead of going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos explaining the same concepts better.
Q10: Pretty often, actually. I'd be lying in bed listening to a lecture recording I'd downloaded, taking mental notes, but the LMS would have no idea. Or I'd spend hours going through my handwritten notes before an exam. There was a lot of learning happening that was completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture, listen to it once online, and then replay it offline maybe three or four more times over the next week. The LMS probably thought I only engaged with it once, but I was actually going over the material repeatedly. It was just more convenient to have it saved locally.
Q12: Mid-semester was really rough. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt paralyzed by how much I'd fallen behind. I'd open the LMS, see all the things I hadn't done, and just close it again because it was overwhelming. The peer support helped a bit, but there were weeks where I could barely function academically.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd prioritize sleep or just zoning out because I was running on empty. Having 18 courses worth of content to manage was insane, and sometimes I'd just shut down completely. Competing responsibilities like work and personal stuff made it worse.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd finish watching something and realize I had no idea what it was about.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start things because I was afraid of doing them wrong. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It was this awful cycle of avoidance that I couldn't break out of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this massive spike of activity right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I'd just drift. The LMS probably shows this really clearly in my login patterns.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. Sometimes getting a decent grade would motivate me for a day or two. But if the feedback was critical or I did poorly, it would make my anxiety worse and I'd avoid the course for a while. It's complicated because I needed the feedback but also dreaded it.
Q18: The structure was honestly overwhelming with so many courses. I couldn't keep track of what was due when across everything. If the course had been more linear or had clearer weekly goals, maybe I would have engaged more consistently. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually made it easier for me to procrastinate.
Q19: Having audio options made things easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the sheer volume of content and the multiple platforms we had to use made engagement harder. Also, the assignments weren't always clear about expectations, which increased my anxiety about starting them.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious like me, checking things repeatedly but too scared to actually submit work. Or maybe they're genuinely trying to understand but struggling, so they keep reviewing without making progress. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, that's for sure.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I did okay on exams because I studied a lot offline, listening to recordings and reviewing my notes. The LMS didn't capture most of my actual studying. Someone could look at my low login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but that's not the full picture.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging. They see actions but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, engagement would mean being curious and motivated, not just stressed and obligated. I don't think I was truly engaged most of the time, honestly.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything, or how the peer support program was sometimes the only thing keeping me connected to the course at all. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while trying to fall asleep, or how hard I was trying even when it didn't look like it. There's a whole emotional dimension that data just can't capture.
